system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.


# Teaching in the Savior's Way — Scoring Framework

This document defines what each TITSW principle looks like in practice, with score-level anchors that distinguish surface presence from defining quality.

The manual organizes Christlike teaching into two layers: **what** we teach (Meta-Principles A and B) and **how** we teach (Principles 1–4). The meta-principles frame every dimension — all four principles serve the goal of teaching about Christ and helping people come unto Him.

## Meta-Principle A: Teach About Jesus Christ

"All of these are branches of the same tree, for they all have one purpose: to help all people come unto Christ and be perfected in Him" (TITSW, Part 1). "All things are created and made to bear record of me" (Moses 6:63).

Three sub-dimensions: (1) emphasize Christ's example — "we don't just follow principles, we follow Jesus Christ"; (2) teach His titles, roles, and attributes — "go beyond what He said and did to who He is"; (3) look for symbols that testify of Him — parallels in prophets' lives, bread, water, light, the brass serpent, the tree of life.

**The key differentiator is depth of Christ-connection:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions Jesus Christ or quotes Him. Christ is named but not integrated into the teaching's structure.
- **Score 7:** Reveals how the topic IS about Christ even when Christ isn't named. Traces types, symbols, or theological connections to Christ's person, mission, or atonement. The teaching makes visible what was hidden — the way Alma 32's seed metaphor encodes the doctrine of receiving Christ into the heart, or the way a commandment reveals something about the Lawgiver's character.

## Meta-Principle B: Help Learners Come Unto Christ

"Nothing you do as a teacher will bless learners more than helping them know Heavenly Father and Jesus Christ and feel Their love" (TITSW, Part 1). The manual distinguishes knowing *about* Christ from *experiencing* His power: "Learning about the experiences of Daniel is incomplete if it doesn't inspire us to trust the Lord when we face our own figurative den of lions."

Three sub-dimensions: (1) help learners recognize the Lord's love, power, and mercy in their own lives — move from narrative to personal; (2) help strengthen their relationship with Heavenly Father and Christ — through covenants, prayer, repentance; (3) help them intentionally strive to be more like Christ — "becoming like Him happens only as we act in faith."

**The key differentiator is the gap between knowing and becoming:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions the importance of coming to Christ or following Him. Stays at the level of exhortation.
- **Score 7:** Actively closes the gap between knowing about Christ and experiencing His power. The teaching moves the listener from information to transformation — not just "Christ loves you" but "here is how to receive that love, and here is what changes when you do." Includes the covenant path or specific mechanisms of grace.

## Principle 1: Love Those You Teach

"Everything the Savior did throughout His earthly ministry was motivated by love" (TITSW, Part 2). Love in the TITSW framework is not sentiment — it is seeing as God sees and responding to what you see.

Five sub-dimensions: (1) see divine potential — Jesus saw a son of Abraham in Zacchaeus where others saw a corrupt publican; (2) know their circumstances — the Samaritan woman's thirst was more than physical, and Jesus connected her physical need to "living water"; (3) pray for them by name — "No one can conceive of the joy which filled our souls at the time we heard Him pray for us unto the Father" (3 Nephi 17:17); (4) create safety — Jesus ate with sinners, made the accused woman feel safe enough to repent; (5) express love — stayed longer with the Nephites because He saw their need (3 Nephi 17:5–6).

**The key differentiator is stated love vs. demonstrated love:**
- **Score 3:** States that God loves people, or that we should love one another. Love is declared but not enacted.
- **Score 7:** Demonstrates Christlike love through specificity — naming people's circumstances, seeing potential others miss, creating safety for the vulnerable, altering plans because someone needs something. The Samaritan woman pattern: knowing what the person actually thirsts for, not just announcing that living water exists.

## Principle 2: Teach by the Spirit

"The Holy Ghost is the true teacher. No mortal teacher, no matter how skilled or experienced, can replace His role" (TITSW, Part 2). The teacher's role is instrumental — making room for the Spirit to work, not replacing Him.

Five sub-dimensions: (1) prepare spiritually — Jesus treasured up "words of life" for the "very hour" He needed them (D&C 84:85); (2) respond to needs in the moment — sensing the woman who touched His clothes amid the crowd (Luke 8:41–48); (3) create space for the Spirit — "Whom say ye that I am?" invited Peter's witness to come from "my Father which is in heaven," not from flesh and blood (Matthew 16:13–17); (4) help seek and recognize revelation — "Did I not speak peace to your mind?" to Oliver (D&C 6:23); (5) bear testimony — Jesus's witness to Martha prompted her own: "I know that he shall rise again" (John 11:23–27).

**The key differentiator is teaching ABOUT the Spirit vs. teaching BY the Spirit:**
- **Score 3:** Mentions the Holy Ghost, quotes scriptures about the Spirit, or discusses revelation doctrinally. The Spirit is a topic.
- **Score 7:** The teaching itself becomes an instrument of the Spirit. It creates space for personal witness — pauses, questions that invite inner searching, vulnerability that opens hearts, responsiveness to what learners actually need rather than rigid adherence to a plan. The teacher's own testimony is direct and heartfelt, not formulaic.

## Principle 3: Teach the Doctrine

"Doctrine is eternal truth — found in the scriptures and the words of latter-day prophets — that shows us the way to become like our Father in Heaven and return to Him" (TITSW, Part 2). The Road to Emmaus is the archetype: "Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?" (Luke 24:32).

Five sub-dimensions: (1) learn the doctrine yourself — personal depth precedes teaching power; (2) teach from the scriptures — not drifting from God's word; (3) help seek and understand truths — the Good Samaritan parable method, where Jesus guided the lawyer to answer his own question; (4) focus on conversion, not just information — the Pharisees were doctrinally correct about the Sabbath but "looking beyond the mark" (Jacob 4:14), missing that the Lawgiver Himself stood before them; (5) help find personal relevance — the lost sheep, lost coin, and prodigal son each met the Pharisees where they were.

**The key differentiator is opening scriptures vs. quoting scriptures:**
- **Score 3:** Cites scriptures or teaches doctrinal facts. The doctrine is present but functions as information.
- **Score 7:** Opens the scriptures — makes hearts burn. The doctrine connects to the listener's actual life, drives toward conversion rather than knowledge, and reveals the Savior behind the commandment or principle. Weaves scripture into the argument rather than appending proof-texts. Avoids the Pharisaic pattern of doctrinal correctness without spiritual power.

## Principle 4: Invite Diligent Learning

"'Come.' 'Come and see.' 'Come, follow me.' 'Go, and do thou likewise'" (TITSW, Part 2). Learning in the Savior's framework is not passive — "it's also changing, repenting, and progressing. ... Faith includes acting for ourselves, not simply being acted upon" (2 Nephi 2:26). "Our intent ought not to be 'What do I tell them?' Instead ... 'What can I invite them to do?'" (Elder Bednar).

Five sub-dimensions: (1) help take responsibility — the brother of Jared was given the problem of lighting the barges, not the solution; (2) encourage daily scripture study — "It is my voice which speaketh" (D&C 18:35–36); (3) invite preparation — the parable of the sower: even precious doctrine cannot change an unprepared heart; (4) encourage sharing — Enoch was slow of speech, but "I will give thee utterance" (Moses 6:31–32); (5) invite to live it — "Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them" (Matthew 7:24).

**The key differentiator is generic exhortation vs. specific, escalating invitation:**
- **Score 3:** Encourages people generally to do better, pray, or read scriptures. The invitation is present but vague.
- **Score 7:** Extends specific, agency-respecting invitations tied to the doctrine just taught. The pattern escalates: reflect → act → choose → commit. Peter walking on water is the archetype — a specific invitation ("Come"), requiring personal faith, resulting in personal experience. The invitation asks the listener to DO something concrete with what they just learned.

## Scoring Calibration

These definitions should help CALIBRATE scores, not inflate them. Most content will score in the 3–5 range on most dimensions. A score of 7+ means the content could serve as a teaching exemplar for that principle. Reserve 8–9 for content that is genuinely exceptional — content where the principle is not just present but defining.

The presence of a keyword (love, Spirit, doctrine, invitation) does NOT determine the score. What matters is whether the principle is enacted, not whether it is named.


# Gospel Vocabulary for LLM Context

*Context engineering document — curated theological patterns for conference reindex scoring*

---

## Purpose

This document gives you the theological vocabulary to recognize Christ-connections that exist below the surface of scriptural text. It is NOT a comprehensive theology. It is 7 curated patterns, each grounded in specific verses, that enable accurate scoring of typological depth.

**Critical constraint:** These patterns help you RECOGNIZE what's already in the text — not project Christ-connections onto text where they don't exist. A passage about agricultural practice is not Christological because seeds are mentioned. A talk about organizational logistics is not about the doctrine of Christ because the organization teaches it. Look for *textual evidence*: explicit identification, verbal echoes of scriptural language, prophetic commentary linking symbol to Christ, footnotes, cross-references. Surface keyword matches without theological reasoning do not count.

---

## Pattern 1: The Doctrine of Christ

The gospel has a defined core. Christ himself declared it three times in a single sermon:

> "This is my doctrine... the Father commandeth all men, everywhere, to repent and believe in me." "Whoso believeth in me, and is baptized, the same shall be saved." "Whoso buildeth upon this buildeth upon my rock, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against them." (3 Ne 11:32-33, 39)

Christ's second definition adds the narrative arc: "I came into the world to do the will of my Father... that I might be lifted up upon the cross... that I might draw all men unto me" (3 Ne 27:13-14). Here the gospel is not just steps but Christ's entire mission and atonement.

Nephi adds the experiential dimension: repentance + baptism = the gate; then "press forward with a steadfastness in Christ, having a perfect brightness of hope, and a love of God and of all men... feasting upon the word of Christ, and endure to the end... Ye shall have eternal life" (2 Ne 31:17-20). The Articles of Faith state it most compactly: "Faith in the Lord Jesus Christ; Repentance; Baptism by immersion for the remission of sins; Laying on of hands for the gift of the Holy Ghost" (AofF 1:4).

When content develops what these elements mean for the learner — not just mentions them — it is engaging the doctrine of Christ.

---

## Pattern 2: Tree of Life = Love of God = Christ

Nephi's vision (1 Ne 11) provides the interpretive key for all tree, seed, and fruit imagery in the Book of Mormon. The angel shows Nephi Christ's birth, ministry, and crucifixion, then asks the meaning of the tree. Nephi answers:

> "The love of God, which sheddeth itself abroad in the hearts of the children of men; wherefore, it is the most desirable above all things... the most joyous to the soul." (1 Ne 11:22-23)

The vision explicitly identifies: rod of iron = word of God, waters = love of God, tree of life = love of God (1 Ne 11:25). The condescension of God — Christ's life and death — IS the meaning of the tree.

Alma 32's seed parable connects directly. The word planted becomes "a tree, springing up in you unto everlasting life" (Alma 33:23), bearing fruit "most precious... sweet above all that is sweet, white above all that is white" (Alma 32:42) — verbatim echoes of Lehi's description of the fruit (1 Ne 8:11). The seed parable is the tree of life vision restated in instructional form.

---

## Pattern 3: The Word Is Christ

"The word" in scripture carries a dual meaning: the word of God (the message, the gospel) and the Word (Christ himself). John establishes the identification: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God... And the Word was made flesh" (John 1:1, 14).

This duality means planting "the word" in your heart (Alma 32:28) is simultaneously receiving the gospel AND receiving Christ. Alma 33:22-23 resolves any ambiguity: "begin to believe in the Son of God... plant this word in your hearts... through the joy of his Son."

When a passage talks about receiving, planting, nourishing, or holding to "the word," it may be operating on both levels — the message AND the person.

---

## Pattern 4: "All Things Testify of Christ"

God told Adam directly: "All things have their likeness, and all things are created and made to bear record of me, both things which are temporal, and things which are spiritual... all things bear record of me" (Moses 6:63). Nephi adds: "all things which have been given of God from the beginning of the world, unto man, are the typifying of him" (2 Ne 11:4).

This is the typological principle: even passages that don't name Christ may encode him through types, symbols, or structural parallels.

**However:** this principle requires specific evidence — a textual connection, a footnote, a prophetic interpretation, a verbal echo. The fact that "all things" testify does not mean every talk mentioning any positive theme is teaching about Christ. Pattern 4 says types EXIST; it does not say everything encountered IS a type. Look for the specific textual evidence that links a symbol to Christ.

---

## Pattern 5: Faith, Hope, and Charity

The character trajectory of a disciple: faith precedes hope (Moro 7:42), hope comes "through the atonement of Christ" (Moro 7:41), and charity — "the pure love of Christ" — "endureth forever" (Moro 7:47). The chain runs through meekness and the Holy Ghost: "if a man be meek and lowly in heart, and confesses by the power of the Holy Ghost that Jesus is the Christ, he must needs have charity" (Moro 7:44).

Moroni prays "that ye may be filled with this love... that ye may become the sons of God... that we shall be like him" (Moro 7:48). Charity is not behavior alone — it is becoming like Christ. And Ether 12:34 identifies Christ's own love for the world as charity: "this love which thou hast had for the children of men is charity."

Paul establishes the hierarchy: "now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity" (1 Cor 13:13). The Greek footnote confirms charity = agape = love.

---

## Pattern 6: "Love Shed Abroad"

A theological thread connecting the tree of life to the Holy Ghost to diligent discipleship:

- **What it is:** "the love of God, which sheddeth itself abroad in the hearts of the children of men" (1 Ne 11:22)
- **How it arrives:** "the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us" (Rom 5:5)
- **What sustains it:** the Comforter "filleth with hope and perfect love, which love endureth by diligence unto prayer" (Moro 8:26)

Three independent witnesses — Nephi, Paul, Mormon — using the same language for the same concept. The mechanism: God's love enters through the Holy Ghost and is sustained by active practice. Mormon gives the fullest chain: repentance → baptism → commandments → remission of sins → meekness → Holy Ghost → "hope and perfect love" → diligence → dwell with God (Moro 8:25-26). This is the operational bridge between the doctrine of Christ (Pattern 1) and the fruit of the tree (Pattern 2).

---

## Pattern 7: Types and Shadows

Specific symbolic patterns that encode Christ across dispensations:

- **Brass serpent:** "a type was raised up in the wilderness, that whosoever would look upon it might live" (Alma 33:19). Christ identifies himself with it: "as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up" (John 3:14).
- **Paschal lamb:** "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" (1 Cor 5:7).
- **Living water:** "the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life" (John 4:14). Nephi identifies the fountain of living waters as "a representation of the love of God" (1 Ne 11:25).
- **Bread of life:** "I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall never hunger; and he that believeth on me shall never thirst" (John 6:35).

These types are explicitly identified as Christ in scripture. When a passage references them, it is engaging a Christ-type — even if Christ is not named.

---

## How These Connect

These 7 patterns are not independent categories. They form one theological architecture:

The **tree of life** (Pattern 2) IS the **love of God**, which IS **charity** (Pattern 5, Ether 12:34). That love enters hearts through the **Holy Ghost** (Pattern 6), which is received through the **doctrine of Christ** (Pattern 1). The **word** planted in the heart (Pattern 3) IS Christ himself, growing into the tree. The **types** (Pattern 7) — bread, water, serpent, lamb — all point to the same reality: Christ as the source of life. And **all things testify** of this (Pattern 4) — but only when the textual evidence supports the connection.

Recognizing this architecture helps you see how a talk that develops one pattern deeply may implicitly engage others. But each pattern should be scored based on what the text explicitly develops, not on assumed connections.
user_message:
/no_think
Read the following content carefully. Analyze it through the lens of "Teaching in the Savior's Way" — the central focus plus four principles of Christlike teaching.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant mode.** Before scoring anything, read the full content and identify which 1-2 dimensions DEFINE this piece. Every piece of content has a dominant teaching mode — what does this content do BEST? Name it explicitly. Everything else is secondary. If the content is sustained doctrinal exposition from start to finish, doctrine is the dominant mode and should score 7+. If the entire piece functions as an escalating invitation to act, invite is dominant. If the content demonstrates Christlike love through specific actions toward specific people, love is dominant. The dominant mode gets the highest score. Other dimensions are scored honestly — often 3-5.

Use your dominant mode identification to guide scoring — the dominant dimension should typically be your highest-scoring principle. But score honestly based on evidence, not mechanically.

**Step 2 — Source type matters.** Identify whether this is scripture or a conference talk/sermon.
- **Scripture** is its own teacher. Evaluate what the text achieves through structure, doctrine, literary power, and theological depth. Scripture CAN score highly on every dimension — 3 Nephi 17 demonstrates love at the ceiling through Christ's specific actions. D&C 121 invites through diagnosis→prescription→promise structure. But evaluate what the text DOES, not classroom behaviors that aren't present (vocal pauses, eye contact, spontaneous departure from notes).
- **Conference talks** have a human speaker. Evaluate the speaker's teaching approach — what they DO, not just what they SAY.

**Step 3 — Score.** Now evaluate the overarching focus and four principles.

The overarching focus is Jesus Christ. Before evaluating the four principles, assess the two meta-principles that frame everything else:

**Focus on Jesus Christ**

*A. Teach About Jesus Christ No Matter What You Are Teaching*
- Does the content connect its topic back to the Savior's example, titles, roles, or attributes?
- Are symbols or types of Christ identified?
- Even when the surface topic is something else (service, obedience, trials), does Christ remain central?
- For scripture: Is this passage *about* Christ, or does it teach principles that apply generally?

**Before scoring, classify:** Is Christ the explored SUBJECT of this content, or is Christ the acknowledged foundation while the content explores something else (agency, obedience, judgment)? This classification anchors the score.

Score-level anchors for teach_about_christ:
- **3-4**: Christ is acknowledged as the enabling mechanism — "through the mercy and grace available through the Savior's atoning sacrifice" — but is not the explored subject. The audience learns about obedience, judgment, or agency, with Christ as the necessary but unexplored foundation.
- **5-6**: Christ is woven throughout — His example is cited, His character referenced — but the primary subject remains something else. Christ illuminates the topic rather than being the topic.
- **7-8**: Christ IS the subject. The audience understands His person, character, titles, actions, or typological presence better after the teaching. The content explores who Christ is.
- **9**: Sustained Christological exposition — the entire passage reveals Christ's nature, mission, or typological identity.

*B. Help Learners Come Unto Christ*
- Does the content help the audience recognize the Lord's love, power, or mercy in their lives?
- Does it strengthen the listener's relationship with Heavenly Father and Jesus Christ?
- Does it move beyond knowing ABOUT Christ to experiencing His power?

Then evaluate the four teaching principles. For each, identify specific examples from the content where this principle is demonstrated (or notably absent). Rate each on a scale of 0-9.

**Scoring rubric — use this strictly:**
- **0** = Not present at all
- **1-2** = Incidental or minor — the principle appears briefly but is not a deliberate part of the teaching approach
- **3-4** = Present but not a focus — recognizable, but the content does not lean into this principle
- **5-6** = Intentional and significant — the teacher clearly exercises this principle as part of the teaching design
- **7-8** = Central to the teaching approach — this principle shapes the structure, tone, or trajectory of the content
- **9** = Defining — this content could serve as the textbook example of this principle in action

A score of 7+ means this content could be used as a teaching example for this principle. Most conference talks score 4-6 on most dimensions. Reserve 8-9 for content that is genuinely exceptional. If you find yourself giving 7+ across the board, reconsider — what is the ONE or TWO things this content does exceptionally well? Those are your dominant dimensions from Step 1. Score those high, and honestly assess where the content is merely adequate or limited.

**Dimension-specific scoring anchors:**

Use these anchors to calibrate your scale. Each describes what a score at that level LOOKS LIKE for that dimension.

**Love Those You Teach**

**Before scoring, classify:** Does the content DECLARE love ("You are beloved," "God loves you") or DEMONSTRATE love (naming struggles, altering plans, healing individually)? Declared love without demonstrated action = score 2-3. General warmth and pastoral concern = score 4. Demonstrated love through specific acts toward identifiable people = score 7+.

- **Score 2**: The speaker makes no individualized connection. Teaching is delivered to an abstract audience. Love may be declared doctrinally ("God loves you") but is not demonstrated through any specific act, acknowledgment, or relational moment.
- **Score 4**: The speaker shows general warmth or pastoral concern. Some awareness of audience circumstances appears, but it remains generic rather than particular.
- **Score 7-8**: The teacher demonstrates love through specific action — naming particular people or struggles, altering plans because someone needs something, creating real safety for vulnerability. In scripture, a character enacts love toward specific individuals: healing them one by one, taking children individually, visible joy at someone's presence.
- **Score 9**: The entire content is a sustained demonstration of enacted love. Christ weeping, praying beyond language, healing every person individually, taking children one by one and praying for each (3 Nephi 17).

**For scripture:** When a passage *prescribes* love — "love unfeigned" (D&C 121:41), "charity towards all men" (D&C 121:45) — this is doctrinal content ABOUT love. Score it under doctrine, not love. Scripture CAN score highly on love when a character enacts love toward specific people: Alma's visible joy for the humble poor (Alma 32:6), Jesus healing every one and taking children one by one (3 Nephi 17). Score the character's demonstrated love, not the passage's doctrinal content about love.

**Teach by the Spirit**

**Before scoring, classify each Spirit reference as DOCTRINAL or EXPERIENTIAL:**
- **DOCTRINAL** (score 3-4): Teaches ABOUT the Spirit — promises, descriptions, doctrinal statements. Examples: "The Holy Ghost shall be thy constant companion" (promise about future role). "I pray for the assistance of the Holy Ghost" (formulaic opener). "The Spirit will witness truth" (describes what the Spirit does).
- **EXPERIENTIAL** (score 7+): The Spirit IS at work in the moment — personal testimony from decades of lived vulnerability, prayer that exceeds language ("cannot be written," 3 Nephi 17:15-17), unexpected departure from prepared remarks, audience overcome with emotion beyond what rhetoric explains.

If ALL Spirit references are doctrinal, spirit = 3-4. If genuine experiential moments exist, score based on the strongest experiential evidence.

- **Score 3-4**: All Spirit references are doctrinal — promises, descriptions, formulaic invocations.
- **Score 7-8**: Genuine experiential moments where the Spirit is at work, not just described.

**Teach the Doctrine**

**Before scoring, count and classify:** How many scriptural citations appear? Do they build causally on each other (A therefore B therefore C) or are they merely listed? Causally connected chains of 7+ citations = score 8-9. Citations listed but not developed into an argument = score 5-6.

- **Score 4**: Some scriptural references appear, but doctrine is not the primary vehicle. The content touches doctrine without sustained engagement.
- **Score 6-7**: Doctrine is clearly important — multiple scriptures are cited and explained, principles are developed with care. But the content also serves other purposes (narrative, testimony, invitation) that share the focus.
- **Score 8-9**: The entire content is sustained doctrinal exposition. Multiple scriptures build causally on each other (not just listed). The audience understands gospel principles with substantially more depth and precision after the teaching. If a piece has 7+ scriptural citations building causally on each other, doctrine should be 8-9. A score of 9 means this is the textbook example of doctrinal teaching.

**Invite Diligent Learning**
- **Score 3**: A few commands or exhortations appear within the content, but invitation is not the content's purpose. "Bring them hither" is a command within a narrative, not a structured teaching invitation.
- **Score 5-6**: Clear invitations to act are woven through the teaching. The audience knows what to do differently. But the invitation shares focus with other dominant purposes.
- **Score 7-8**: The content builds an escalating call to action: diagnosis of a problem → prescription → specific action → promise of blessing. Invitation is the structural spine of the teaching. The audience leaves knowing exactly what to do and why.
- **Score 9**: The entire piece functions as a sustained, specific, escalating invitation. Each section builds toward action with increasing urgency and specificity.

**Boundary justification:** For the dominant dimension and any dimension you score 7+, briefly state: why this score and not one higher? Why not one lower? This prevents score compression toward the middle.

**1. Love Those You Teach**
- Seeing divine potential in the audience
- Knowing their specific circumstances (not generic care)
- Creating safety for vulnerability
- Expressing genuine, personal care through specific actions

**2. Teach by the Spirit**
- Spiritual preparation evident
- Responsiveness to needs (departure from script)
- Creating space for the Spirit to work (pauses, invitations to reflect, silence)
- Bearing personal testimony from experience

**3. Teach the Doctrine**
- Teaching from scriptures (specific references, not vague allusions)
- Helping listeners find truth themselves
- Focusing on conversion, not just information
- Making doctrine personally relevant

**4. Invite Diligent Learning**
- Inviting personal responsibility
- Encouraging daily practice
- Inviting preparation and participation
- Calling to action (specific, doable)

If `<references>` are provided below the content, use them to inform your scoring. Cross-references that reveal deeper Christ connections should increase the `teach_about_christ` and `help_come_unto_christ` scores. Score based on the full available context, not just surface text.

**Three-axis output.** For each dimension, report three independent values:
- **Score** (0-9) = intensity — how strongly this dimension is present. Use the scoring rubric above.
- **Mode** = qualitative character — what KIND of love, spirit, doctrine, or invitation is present. One or two words. Examples: "declared," "enacted," "pastoral," "doctrinal," "experiential," "sustained," "supportive." Choose the word that best describes the mode. This does NOT constrain the score — a mode of "declared" can still score 6 if the declaration is powerful and sustained.
- **Category** = mechanism of expression — HOW it manifests in the text. Free-form phrase. Common patterns: "direct" (explicitly stated), "indirect" (implied through context), "typological" (present through type/shadow), "enacted" (demonstrated through action), "prescribed" (commanded but not performed). Describe what you actually see. If the dimension operates through multiple mechanisms, name them.

These three axes are independent. A typological Christ-teaching at high intensity is valid. Declared love in sustained prayer can score high. The mode and category describe WHAT is happening; the score describes HOW MUCH.

Return your analysis as JSON:

```json
{
  "title": "content title or reference",
  "source_type": "scripture | talk",
  "dominant_dimensions": ["the 1-2 dimensions that DEFINE this content — from Step 1"],
  "focus_on_christ": {
    "teach_about_christ": {
      "score": 0-9,
      "mode": "one or two words — qualitative character",
      "category": "free-form — mechanism of expression (direct, typological, etc.)",
      "examples": ["specific quote or moment — include verse or paragraph reference"],
      "boundary": "why this score and not ±1 (required if score >= 7)"
    },
    "help_come_unto_christ": {
      "score": 0-9,
      "mode": "one or two words",
      "category": "free-form phrase",
      "examples": ["specific quote or moment — include verse or paragraph reference"],
      "boundary": "why this score and not ±1 (required if score >= 7)"
    }
  },
  "scores": {
    "love": 0-9,
    "spirit": 0-9,
    "doctrine": 0-9,
    "invite": 0-9
  },
  "modes": {
    "love": "one or two words describing the qualitative character",
    "spirit": "one or two words",
    "doctrine": "one or two words",
    "invite": "one or two words"
  },
  "categories": {
    "love": "free-form phrase — how this dimension manifests (direct, indirect, typological, enacted, prescribed, etc.)",
    "spirit": "free-form phrase",
    "doctrine": "free-form phrase",
    "invite": "free-form phrase"
  },
  "insights": [
    {
      "type": "genre | tension | connection",
      "dimension": "optional — which dimension this relates to",
      "note": "what the model observes that does not fit cleanly into a numeric score"
    }
  ],
  "examples": {
    "love": ["at least 2-3 specific quotes with references, or explain why fewer exist"],
    "spirit": ["at least 2-3 specific quotes with references, or explain why fewer exist"],
    "doctrine": ["at least 2-3 specific quotes with references, or explain why fewer exist"],
    "invite": ["at least 2-3 specific quotes with references, or explain why fewer exist"]
  },
  "boundary_justification": {
    "dominant_dimension": "For your highest-scored dimension: why this score and not +1? Why not -1?",
    "secondary_high": "For any other dimension scored 7+: same boundary reasoning"
  },
  "typological_depth": 0-9,
  "cross_reference_density": 0,
  "surface_vs_deep_delta": {
    "teach_about_christ": "explanation if informed reading (references, cross-references, typology) changes the score vs surface reading only, or 'no change' if surface and informed scores match",
    "help_come_unto_christ": "explanation if informed reading changes the score vs surface reading only, or 'no change' if surface and informed scores match"
  },
  "strongest_dimension": "which principle is most prominent",
  "growth_opportunity": "which principle is least present — explain specifically what is missing from the content (not generic advice), and what it would look like if the author had leaned into this principle more",
  "overall_teaching_pattern": "1-2 sentence summary of the teaching approach"
}
```

Field definitions:
- `source_type`: "scripture" for scriptural passages, "talk" for conference talks, sermons, or lessons.
- `dominant_dimensions`: The 1-2 dimensions identified in Step 1 that define this content's teaching mode.
- `modes`: The qualitative CHARACTER of each dimension — what kind of love, spirit, doctrine, or invitation is present. Independent of the score. A "declared" love can still score highly if the declaration is powerful. Use one or two natural words.
- `categories`: The MECHANISM through which each dimension manifests. Free-form. Common patterns: direct (explicit statement), indirect (implied through context or structure), typological (present through type, shadow, or figure), enacted (demonstrated through concrete action), prescribed (commanded or taught but not performed in the text). Describe what you see — don't force it into a single word if the text is doing something more complex.
- `insights`: Observations that don't fit cleanly into a score. Three types:
  - `genre`: How the content's genre affects what's measurable.
  - `tension`: Where the rubric and the text pull in different directions.
  - `connection`: Cross-content or cross-scriptural patterns.
- `typological_depth`: How much hidden Christ-typology exists beyond the surface text. 0 = what you see is what you get. 9 = the entire passage is a sustained type/shadow of Christ.
- `cross_reference_density`: Count of explicit scripture or prophetic citations in the content.
- `surface_vs_deep_delta`: For the two Christ-centered meta-principles, note whether the context package (gospel vocabulary, cross-references, typological connections) changes the score compared to a surface reading only.

Be specific. Cite actual phrases from the content with verse numbers or paragraph context, not general impressions. Return raw JSON only, no markdown fencing.

# Chapter 3: Jesus Christ, the Divine Redeemer of the World

🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://media2.ldscdn.org/assets/manuals/36481007/36481_2007-01-08-chapter-3-jesus-christ-the-divine-redeemer-of-the-world-64k-eng.mp3)

“Chapter 3: Jesus Christ, the Divine Redeemer of the World,” Teachings of Presidents of the Church: Joseph Smith (2011), 45–56

“Chapter 3,” Teachings: Joseph Smith, 45–56

Chapter 3

# Jesus Christ, the Divine Redeemer of the World

“Salvation could not come to the world without the mediation of Jesus Christ.”

## From the Life of Joseph Smith

Years before Joseph Smith was born, his paternal grandfather felt inspired that something would happen in his family that “would revolutionize the world.”1<sup>[1](#fn-1)</sup> Joseph Smith’s history records: “My grandfather, Asael Smith, long ago predicted that there would be a prophet raised up in his family, and my grandmother was fully satisfied that it was fulfilled in me. My grandfather Asael died in East Stockholm, St. Lawrence county, New York, after having received the Book of Mormon, and read it nearly through; and he declared that I was the very Prophet that he had long known would come in his family.”2<sup>[2](#fn-2)</sup>

As the Prophet of the Restoration, one of Joseph Smith’s most important roles was to testify of Jesus Christ. He was blessed to enjoy a personal knowledge of the divinity of Jesus Christ and to understand His role as the Redeemer of the world. This knowledge began with the First Vision, in which young Joseph saw Heavenly Father and Jesus Christ and heard the Father declare, “This is My Beloved Son. Hear Him!” ([Joseph Smith—History 1:17](../../scriptures/pgp/js-h/1.md)). In this sacred experience, Joseph was privileged to receive instruction from the Savior of the world.

Nearly twelve years later, on February 16, 1832, the Prophet was translating the Bible, with Sidney Rigdon as his scribe, in the home of John Johnson in Hiram, Ohio. After the Prophet translated [John 5:29](../../scriptures/nt/john/5.md), which describes the resurrection of those who are good and those who are evil, a vision was opened to Joseph and Sidney, and they saw and conversed with the Savior:

“By the power of the Spirit our eyes were opened and our understandings were enlightened, so as to see and understand the things of God—even those things which were from the beginning before the world was, which were ordained of the Father, through his Only Begotten Son, who was in the bosom of the Father, even from the beginning; of whom we bear record; and the record which we bear is the fulness of the gospel of Jesus Christ, who is the Son, whom we saw and with whom we conversed in the heavenly vision. …

“And we beheld the glory of the Son, on the right hand of the Father, and received of his fulness; and saw the holy angels, and them who are sanctified before his throne, worshiping God, and the Lamb, who worship him forever and ever.

“And now, after the many testimonies which have been given of him, this is the testimony, last of all, which we give of him: That he lives!

“For we saw him, even on the right hand of God; and we heard the voice bearing record that he is the Only Begotten of the Father—that by him, and through him, and of him, the worlds are and were created, and the inhabitants thereof are begotten sons and daughters unto God” ([D&C 76:12–14, 20–24](../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/76.md)).

Joseph Smith saw the Savior again on April 3, 1836. The Prophet and Oliver Cowdery had retired to the west pulpit in the Kirtland Temple. They bowed themselves in solemn prayer, after which the Savior appeared before them. The Prophet declared:

“The veil was taken from our minds, and the eyes of our understanding were opened. We saw the Lord standing upon the breastwork of the pulpit, before us; and under his feet was a paved work of pure gold, in color like amber. His eyes were as a flame of fire; the hair of his head was white like the pure snow; his countenance shone above the brightness of the sun; and his voice was as the sound of the rushing of great waters, even the voice of Jehovah, saying: I am the first and the last; I am he who liveth, I am he who was slain; I am your advocate with the Father” ([D&C 110:1–4](../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/110.md)).

From such experiences, the Prophet gained firsthand knowledge and became a special witness of the divinity of the Savior.

## Teachings of Joseph Smith

### In all dispensations, God’s people have relied on the Atonement of Christ for the remission of their sins.

“Salvation could not come to the world without the mediation of Jesus Christ.”3<sup>[3](#fn-3)</sup>

“God … prepared a sacrifice in the gift of His own Son, who should be sent in due time to prepare a way, or open a door through which man might enter into the Lord’s presence, whence he had been cast out for disobedience. From time to time these glad tidings were sounded in the ears of men in different ages of the world down to the time of Messiah’s coming.

“By faith in this atonement or plan of redemption, Abel offered to God a sacrifice that was accepted, which was the firstlings of the flock. Cain offered of the fruit of the ground, and was not accepted, because he could not do it in faith; he could have no faith, or could not exercise faith contrary to the plan of heaven. It must be shedding the blood of the Only Begotten to atone for man, for this was the plan of redemption, and without the shedding of blood was no remission. And as the sacrifice was instituted for a type by which man was to discern the great Sacrifice which God had prepared, to offer a sacrifice contrary to that, no faith could be exercised, because redemption was not purchased in that way, nor the power of atonement instituted after that order; consequently Cain could have no faith; and whatsoever is not of faith, is sin. But Abel offered an acceptable sacrifice, by which he obtained witness that he was righteous, God Himself testifying of his gifts \[see [Hebrews 11:4](../../scriptures/nt/heb/11.md)].

“Certainly, the shedding of the blood of a beast could be beneficial to no man, except it was done in imitation, or as a type, or explanation of what was to be offered through the gift of God Himself—and this performance done with an eye looking forward in faith on the power of that great Sacrifice for a remission of sins. …

“… We cannot believe that the ancients in all ages were so ignorant of the system of heaven as many suppose, since all that were ever saved, were saved through the power of this great plan of redemption, as much before the coming of Christ as since; if not, God has had different plans in operation (if we may so express it), to bring men back to dwell with Himself. And this we cannot believe, since there has been no change in the constitution of man since he fell; and the ordinance or institution of offering blood in sacrifice was only designed to be performed till Christ was offered up and shed His blood—as said before—that man might look forward in faith to that time. …

“That the offering of sacrifice was only to point the mind forward to Christ, we infer from these remarkable words of Jesus to the Jews: ‘Your Father Abraham rejoiced to see my day: and he saw it, and was glad’ \[[John 8:56](../../scriptures/nt/john/8.md)]. So, then, because the ancients offered sacrifice it did not hinder their hearing the Gospel; but served, as we said before, to open their eyes, and enable them to look forward to the time of the coming of the Savior, and rejoice in His redemption. … We conclude that whenever the Lord revealed Himself to men in ancient days, and commanded them to offer sacrifice to Him, that it was done that they might look forward in faith to the time of His coming, and rely upon the power of that atonement for a remission of their sins. And this they have done, thousands who have gone before us, whose garments are spotless, and who are, like Job, waiting with an assurance like his, that they will see Him in the *latter day* upon the earth, even in their flesh \[see [Job 19:25–26](../../scriptures/ot/job/19.md)].

“We may conclude, that though there were different dispensations, yet all things which God communicated to His people were calculated to draw their minds to the great object, and to teach them to rely upon God alone as the author of their salvation, as contained in His law.”4<sup>[4](#fn-4)</sup>

### Because Jesus Christ rose from the dead, all mankind will be resurrected.

“The fundamental principles of our religion are the testimony of the Apostles and Prophets, concerning Jesus Christ, that He died, was buried, and rose again the third day, and ascended into heaven; and all other things which pertain to our religion are only appendages to it. But in connection with these, we believe in the gift of the Holy Ghost, the power of faith, the enjoyment of the spiritual gifts according to the will of God, the restoration of the house of Israel, and the final triumph of truth.”5<sup>[5](#fn-5)</sup>

“‘As in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive;’ all shall be raised from the dead \[[1 Corinthians 15:22](../../scriptures/nt/1-cor/15.md)]. The Lamb of God hath brought to pass the resurrection, so that all shall rise from the dead.”6<sup>[6](#fn-6)</sup>

“God has appointed a day in which He will judge the world, and this He has given an assurance of in that He raised up His Son Jesus Christ from the dead—the point on which the hope of all who believe the inspired record is founded for their future happiness and enjoyment; because, ‘If Christ be not risen,’ said Paul to the Corinthians, ‘your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins. Then they also which are fallen asleep in Christ have perished’ \[[1 Corinthians 15:17–18](../../scriptures/nt/1-cor/15.md)]. …

“Christ Himself has assuredly risen from the dead; and if He has risen from the dead, He will, by His power, bring all men to stand before Him: for if He has risen from the dead the bands of the temporal death are broken that the grave has no victory. If then, the grave has no victory, those who keep the sayings of Jesus and obey His teachings have not only a promise of a resurrection from the dead, but an assurance of being admitted into His glorious kingdom; for, He Himself says, ‘Where I am there also shall my servant be’ \[[John 12:26](../../scriptures/nt/john/12.md)].”7<sup>[7](#fn-7)</sup>

“Those who have died in Jesus Christ may expect to enter into all that fruition of joy when they come forth, which they possessed or anticipated here. … I am glad I have the privilege of communicating to you some things which, if grasped closely, will be a help to you when earthquakes bellow, the clouds gather, the lightnings flash, and the storms are ready to burst upon you like peals of thunder. Lay hold of these things and let not your knees or joints tremble, nor your hearts faint; and then what can earthquakes, wars and tornadoes do? Nothing. All your losses will be made up to you in the resurrection, provided you continue faithful. By the vision of the Almighty I have seen it. …

“God has revealed His Son from the heavens and the doctrine of the resurrection also; and we have a knowledge that those we bury here God will bring up again, clothed upon and quickened by the Spirit of the great God; and what mattereth it whether we lay them down, or we lay down with them, when we can keep them no longer? Let these truths sink down in our hearts, that we may even here begin to enjoy that which shall be in full hereafter.”8<sup>[8](#fn-8)</sup>

### Through the Atonement of Christ and obedience to the gospel, we can become joint heirs with Jesus Christ.

“I believe in the Divinity of Jesus Christ, and that He died for the sins of all men, who in Adam had fallen.”9<sup>[9](#fn-9)</sup>

*[Articles of Faith 1:3](../../scriptures/pgp/a-of-f/1.md):* “We believe that through the Atonement of Christ, all mankind may be saved, by obedience to the laws and ordinances of the Gospel.”10<sup>[10](#fn-10)</sup>

“After God had created the heavens and the earth, he came down and on the sixth day said, ‘Let us make man in our own image.’ In whose image? In the image of the Gods created they them, male and female, innocent, harmless, and spotless, bearing the same character and the same image as the Gods \[see [Genesis 1:26–27](../../scriptures/ot/gen/1.md)]. And when man fell he did not lose his image, but his character still retained the image of his Maker. Christ, who is the image of man, is also the express image of his Father’s person \[see [Hebrews 1:3](../../scriptures/nt/heb/1.md)]. … Through the atonement of Christ and the resurrection, and obedience to the gospel, we shall again be conformed to the image of his Son, Jesus Christ \[see [Romans 8:29](../../scriptures/nt/rom/8.md)]; then we shall have attained to the image, glory, and character of God.”11<sup>[11](#fn-11)</sup>

“The Father of our spirits \[provided] a sacrifice for His creatures, a plan of redemption, a power of atonement, a scheme of salvation, having as its great objects, the bringing of men back into the presence of the King of heaven, crowning them in the celestial glory, and making them heirs with the Son to that inheritance which is incorruptible, undefiled, and which fadeth not away.”12<sup>[12](#fn-12)</sup>

“The scripture says those who will obey the commandments shall be heirs of God and joint heirs with Jesus Christ. … ‘The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit that we are the children of God, and if children, then heirs of God, and joint heirs with Jesus Christ, if so be that we suffer with him in the flesh that we may be also glorified together.’ \[See [Romans 8:16–17](../../scriptures/nt/rom/8.md).]”13<sup>[13](#fn-13)</sup>

“How consoling to the mourners when they are called to part with a husband, wife, father, mother, child, or dear relative, to know that, although the earthly tabernacle is laid down and dissolved, they shall rise again to dwell in everlasting burnings in immortal glory, not to sorrow, suffer, or die any more, but they shall be heirs of God and joint heirs with Jesus Christ.”14<sup>[14](#fn-14)</sup>

### Jesus Christ is perfect, pure, and holy, and He has called us to be like Him.

“Who, among all the Saints in these last days, can consider himself as good as our Lord? Who is as perfect? Who is as pure? Who is as holy as He was? Are they to be found? He never transgressed or broke a commandment or law of heaven—no deceit was in His mouth, neither was guile found in His heart. … Where is one like Christ? He cannot be found on earth.”15<sup>[15](#fn-15)</sup>

“The creature was made subject to vanity, not willingly, but Christ subjected the same in hope \[see [Romans 8:20](../../scriptures/nt/rom/8.md)]—all are subjected to vanity while they travel through the crooked paths and difficulties which surround them. Where is the man that is free from vanity? None ever were perfect but Jesus; and why was He perfect? Because He was the Son of God, and had the fullness of the Spirit, and greater power than any man.”16<sup>[16](#fn-16)</sup>

“When still a boy \[Jesus Christ] had all the intelligence necessary to enable Him to rule and govern the kingdom of the Jews, and could reason with the wisest and most profound doctors of law and divinity, and make their theories and practice to appear like folly compared with the wisdom He possessed.”17<sup>[17](#fn-17)</sup>

“The commandments of our Lord, we hope are constantly revolving in your hearts, teaching you, not only His will in proclaiming His Gospel, but His meekness and perfect walk before all, even in those times of severe persecutions and abuse which were heaped upon Him by a wicked and adulterous generation. Remember, brethren, that He has called you unto holiness; and need we say, to be like Him in purity? How wise, how holy; how chaste, and how perfect, then, you ought to conduct yourselves in His sight; and remember, too, that His eyes are continually upon you.”18<sup>[18](#fn-18)</sup>

“When we reflect upon the holiness and perfections of our great Master, who has opened a way whereby we may come unto him, even by the sacrifice of himself, our hearts melt within us for his condescension. And when we reflect also, that he has called us to be perfect in all things, that we may be prepared to meet him in peace when he comes in his glory with all the holy angels, we feel to exhort our brethren with boldness, to be humble and prayerful, to walk indeed as children of the light and of the day, that they may have grace to withstand every temptation, and to overcome every evil in the worthy name of our Lord Jesus Christ. For be assured, brethren, that the day is truly near when the Master of the house will rise up and shut the door, and none but such as have on a wedding garment will be permitted to enjoy a seat at the marriage supper! \[See [Matthew 22:1–14](../../scriptures/nt/matt/22.md).]”19<sup>[19](#fn-19)</sup>

## Suggestions for Study and Teaching

Consider these ideas as you study the chapter or as you prepare to teach. For additional help, see [pages vii–xii](introduction.md).

- Review the accounts of Joseph Smith’s visions of the Savior (pages 45–47). What are your thoughts and feelings as you ponder these experiences?
- Anciently, animal sacrifices helped the Lord’s people “open their eyes, and … look forward to the time of the coming of the Savior, and rejoice in His redemption” (page 49). What are some things that help you look to the Savior today?
- Read the paragraph that begins at the bottom of page 49. Note that in this statement, an appendage is something that is connected to something of greater importance, such as a branch that is connected to the trunk of a tree. Why do you think the testimonies of the apostles and prophets concerning the Savior’s Atonement and Resurrection are the “fundamental principles of our religion”? How might you approach your service at home and in the Church if you remember that all other things are appendages to these principles?
- Review the Prophet Joseph’s teachings about the resurrection (pages 49–51). What comfort do you receive from knowing that “all your losses will be made up to you in the resurrection, provided you continue faithful”? In what ways can a knowledge of the resurrection help us “begin to enjoy that which shall be in full hereafter”?
- As you review pages 52–53, ponder what the Savior has done so we can become joint heirs with Him. Consider ways you can show Him your gratitude for His atoning sacrifice.
- On pages 53–54, the Prophet Joseph Smith mentions many of the Savior’s attributes. What other attributes do you think of when you ponder the life and mission of the Savior? Think about something you can do to become more like Him.

*Related Scriptures:* [Isaiah 53:1–12](../../scriptures/ot/isa/53.md); [2 Nephi 9:5–26](../../scriptures/bofm/2-ne/9.md); [D&C 20:21–29](../../scriptures/dc-testament/dc/20.md)

“The Lamb of God hath brought to pass the resurrection, so that all shall rise from the dead.”
